chapters.title: It's time to break the cycle
chapters.text:
Ever felt stuck in relationships that left you anxious, confused, or just plain miserable? Jessica Baum experienced this firsthand. Before becoming a certified therapist, she spent years dating men who couldn't meet her emotional needs.

In her twenties, she dated a guy who was highly focused on his job and slowly started pulling away. His distance triggered a deep fear of abandonment in her. She felt so anxious that she ended up in the hospital. Her first marriage was no better, as she married someone afraid of emotional connection. Whenever they established closeness, he would withdraw, leaving her feeling alone and panicked.

Identify your attachment style and heal yourself first to break free from toxic relationship cycles.

Jessica didn't have the words to describe her experience. But when she learned she had an anxious attachment style that drove her to seek validation from emotionally unavailable people, her perspective shifted. Once she understood her patterns, she found a partner with whom she built a secure, fulfilling relationship.

Healing doesn't happen by accident — it comes from doing the work to understand our attachment issues and learning to trust ourselves. Sound familiar? It's time to examine your patterns together.

chapters.title: How attachment forms
chapters.text:
Before diving into attachment theory, why not take a quick test to check whether you're anxiously attached? Put down a finger every time you answer "Yes:"

• Do you constantly think about your partner, even at the expense of other interests?
• Do you get anxious when they don't reply to your messages quickly?
• Do you feel disappointed when your partner doesn't meet your expectations?
• Do you try to reconnect immediately after a conflict, unable to let it go?
• Do you check your partner's social media or messages to ensure they aren't hiding something?

If you put four or more fingers down, your attachment style is likely anxious. Now, let's explore what that means in more detail.

Psychologist John Bowlby coined the term "attachment theory" in the 1950s. It suggests that we're wired to connect with others from birth, with our first connection being to our primary caregiver. At that age, we have no idea how important that early bond is. Attachment theory says that how our caregivers respond to our needs as infants directly influences our sense of security in relationships throughout our lives.

Based on these earlier experiences, we develop one of the three main attachment styles: anxious, avoidant, or secure.

1. Those with an anxious attachment style often fear abandonment, leading them to become overly focused on finding relationships to feel secure.
2. Avoidant individuals may fear intimacy and value independence because they learned early on that their emotional needs might not be met.
3. People with a secure attachment style can enter relationships while maintaining healthy boundaries and connections with others.

Researchers Cindy Hazan and Phillip Shaver later expanded on Bowlby's work, noting that around 56% of people have a secure attachment style, 25% are anxiously attached, and 19% are avoidant. These patterns stem from childhood but affect our behavior in romantic relationships.

Healing comes when you learn to balance your needs and respect your boundaries.

Understanding our attachment style is the first step toward creating healthier, more fulfilling connections. Only by recognizing our patterns can we begin to change them. When we start getting to know ourselves better, the relationships we form become opportunities for growth. Remember that true security in love comes from within.

chapters.title: Why unhealthy love is so addictive
chapters.text:
How did Jessica discover she was anxiously attached? She listened to her body's signals. Whenever her partner ghosted her or pulled away, Jessica would experience panic: her gut would sink, her heart would race, and she would do anything to reestablish the connection. She would text repeatedly or apologize without reason, not realizing this was her body's way of expressing that she felt unsafe and abandoned.

Our attachment styles don't just affect our minds — they deeply influence our bodies. Dr. Stephen Porges' polyvagal theory suggests our nervous system constantly scans our environment for safety through a process called neuroception. When we feel safe, we enter the "ventral state" of the autonomic nervous system. Here, our body relaxes: breathing slows, our face softens, and we feel calm, making it easier to connect with others more openly and expressively.

Your body is often smarter than you; listen to it for signals when you need them.

But when we sense danger, the body's fight-or-flight response kicks in. For anxiously attached people, even something small — like a delayed text — can trigger this response. Our heart races, our voice tightens, and we may take actions to keep the connection alive, like chasing or seeking constant reassurance. This scenario also played out in Baum's relationships: her anxious reactions led her partner to pull away even further. In such moments, they both entered an emotional cycle of fear and withdrawal.

So, if you ever felt like this, it wasn't in your head. Before we can process the situation rationally, our nervous system reacts automatically in such situations. Next time you face this, learn to observe your body's signals first. Your racing heart or tight chest doesn't have to control your behavior.

You can use this practice called "heart awareness."

1. Ask yourself, "How am I feeling right now?" and focus on your chest area.
2. Notice your breathing — is it calm or rushed? Try slowing it down.
3. Breathe in for five counts and out for seven counts. Let your stomach expand with each breath.
4. Check your posture. Roll your shoulders back to create space around your heart.
5. Imagine your breath washing over your heart, creating space to open up.

chapters.title: Remember the little you?
chapters.text:
You might think your childhood is over, but we all carry a part of our younger selves — our Little Me — into adulthood. This is the version of ourselves that internalized our emotions and experiences growing up. We all had moments in childhood when our needs weren't met: a parent might have ignored our crying, for example. If repeated often enough, these experiences form core wounds. Our bodies store such memories and shape how we respond to adult relationships.

For those with anxious attachment, the amygdala, which handles emotional responses, becomes hypersensitive to abandonment signals, leading to overreactions in triggering situations. For those with avoidant attachment, core wounds often stem from emotional neglect, creating a protective barrier that shuts down emotional connection when they feel threatened.

Jessica Baum shares the story of her patient, Susan, whose core wound of abandonment showed in her relationship with Dan. Susan often asked Dan to help with the dishes after she cooked dinner. When he forgot, her Little Me immediately felt unappreciated and abandoned. But rather than expressing her needs, Susan bottled up her frustration.

Why? She feared that voicing it would drive Dan even further away. One evening, the built-up tension exploded into an argument as all of Susan's suppressed feelings poured out. This pattern is typical for those with anxious attachment. For people like Susan, the fear of abandonment can lead to avoiding confrontation, which ultimately sabotages relationships.

Take time to reflect on and recognize your core wounds.

So, what's the antidote to these wounds? The answer lies within your Little Me. Connecting with your Little Me helps you acknowledge these wounds and the beliefs they created. Genuine healing lies in internally identifying and addressing these old fears your younger self has held onto.

You can try out this core wound exercise for the start.

1. Write down your core wound (such as, "I will always be left").
2. Reflect on an experience from childhood that first triggered this wound.
3. Identify the belief this wound created (such as "My needs aren't important").
4. Consider how this wound shows up in your current relationships.
5. Notice the physical sensations and emotions that come up (such as sadness or a tight chest).

chapters.title: Why opposites attract
chapters.text:
Just why do people with opposite attachment styles attract like magnets? When they come together, they often trigger each other's deepest fears, creating a push-pull dynamic. Anxiously attached individuals crave closeness, while avoidantly attached partners feel overwhelmed by too much intimacy and withdraw. This cycle can leave both feeling misunderstood in love.

Those with anxious attachment tend to use activating strategies — behaviors designed to restore closeness and connection with their partner when they feel the relationship is at risk. Deep-seated fears of abandonment fuel these strategies.

Some common activating strategies include:

• Sending multiple texts or making repeated calls when their partner doesn't respond immediately.
• Keeping score of how long their partner takes to reply and delaying their responses in return.
• Apologizing excessively, even for things they haven't done, to prevent conflict.
• Pushing for long, intense conversations until they feel reassured.
• Internet stalking — constantly checking their partner's social media for signs of disinterest or betrayal.

Meanwhile, avoidantly attached partners use deactivating strategies to maintain emotional distance and protect themselves from the vulnerability of closeness. These strategies limit intimacy and leave anxious partners feeling even more insecure. Examples of deactivating strategies include:

• Withdrawing and not responding to texts or calls for days, even after an emotional interaction.
• Avoiding emotional conversations or deflecting discussions about the future.
• Keeping a physical distance, such as avoiding affection or not staying over.
• Using work or travel as excuses to create more space.
• Breaking up or ghosting when the relationship becomes too emotionally intense.

You can see how each side reinforces the other's fears.

Become aware of your relationship strategies to break the cycle of unhealthy communication.

Anxiously attached individuals are also particularly vulnerable to narcissists, who use manipulation to control their partners. Narcissists use a tactic called breadcrumbing, offering just enough attention to keep their partner hooked without providing consistent emotional support.

Such intermittent reinforcement keeps the anxious partner always hoping for the next moment of attention. It's akin to betting money in the casino in the hope of finally winning it big one day. Only the prize — the narcissist's love — never comes.

If you're questioning whether you are doomed to maintain the same attachment style, you're not. As we heal and recognize our attachment patterns, the pull toward these unhealthy dynamics fades. With time, we can all prioritize secure, stable relationships that meet our emotional needs and provide genuine safety.

chapters.title: Protect and nurture
chapters.text:
Remember your Little Me? There are players in your psyche who keep them company — your Inner Protectors and Inner Nurturers. These internal voices can help you heal your past wounds and find a healthier balance in life.

Inner Protectors are parts of your psyche that developed to shield you from emotional harm. They act as critical voices or defense mechanisms to keep you safe from repeating painful experiences from your past. For example, if you were punished as a child for making mistakes, you might develop perfectionism as your Inner Protector. It will prevent you from being hurt by others and from being emotionally vulnerable in relationships.

To connect with your Inner Protectors:

• Listen to their voices. Pay attention to thoughts filled with "should" or "shouldn't."
• Notice recurring themes. Are the same issues constantly surfacing, such as perfectionism or fear of abandonment?
• Acknowledge their presence. Thank your protectors for keeping you safe, and reassure them that you're healing.

Inner Nurturers represent the compassionate, loving parts of yourself. They live in the form of people, places, or things that have offered you unconditional support. You have internalized these loving voices, so now they help you feel safe and accepted in challenging times.

Examples of Inner Nurturers include:

• A loving parent or mentor who made you feel seen and valued.
• A close friend who consistently shows empathy and understanding.
• A spiritual figure or higher power that provides a sense of purpose and peace.

Balance your Inner Protectors and Inner Nurturers to create space for your emotional healing and growth.

If you want to cultivate your Inner Nurturers, try this exercise:

1. Close your eyes and picture your chosen Inner Nurturer — someone who offers love and acceptance.
2. Imagine their warmth and kindness flowing into you, and notice how it affects you physically and emotionally.
3. Ask if they have a message for you. Listen carefully for any words, sensations, or emotions that arise.
4. If an Inner Protector appears with criticism or fear, allow your Nurturer to intervene and provide reassurance gently.
5. Thank your Inner Nurturer and Protector for their roles in your journey, acknowledging that healing takes time.

Your Little Me will also benefit if you learn to label your emotions. Dr. Dan Siegel's research shows that labeling your emotions — such as saying "I feel sad" or "I feel anxious" — activates the logical part of the brain. It helps you process emotions more effectively and calm your nervous system.

chapters.title: Becoming self-full
chapters.text:
So, how do you heal, exactly? The answer lies in becoming self-full. It means developing a healthy relationship with yourself, where you recognize and meet your own needs rather than abandoning them for the sake of others. To do that, you must learn to balance caring for yourself and connecting with others.

All your feelings need to know they matter, all our feelings need to be heard. ~ Jessica Baum

Many people, especially those with anxious attachment styles, struggle with this balance. They often prioritize others' emotions over their own, a habit that starts in childhood. For instance, a child might suppress their sadness if it causes their parents to withdraw, leading them to disconnect from their own needs. This pattern carries into adulthood, where people often seek validation from romantic partners, often at the expense of their well-being.

When you're not self-full, you crave constant attention or affection, mistaking it for security.

You can counter these patterns by building healthy boundaries. That doesn't mean shutting people out or giving ultimatums. Instead, it means defining your needs in relationships with others in a respectful way.

Here are some simple steps to start building your boundaries:

• It's okay to press pause when faced with decisions. Give yourself time to reflect before answering to see if it best aligns with your needs.
• Get in touch with your emotions first. Perhaps your unresolved childhood wounds might be acting up, like deciding out of fear of being abandoned.
• Listen to your body; it will signal if something feels right or wrong.
• Communicate your boundaries clearly and calmly, not from a place of guilt or fear.

Another part of being self-full is knowing your non-negotiables in relationships — lines you won't cross. These could be essential boundaries like avoiding physical violence or personal preferences like political alignment. Identify these non-negotiables to build strong interdependent relationships where both partners can grow individually while respecting each other's boundaries.

Did you know? Research on mirror neurons shows how deeply interconnected we are with others. These brain cells activate when we act and observe someone else doing the same in response. For example, if your partner is stressed or anxious, you might feel their emotions like yours. The same goes for parents and children.

chapters.title: From anxious to secure
chapters.text:
As you become more self-full, you create more opportunities to move toward secure relationships. When we progress beyond the honeymoon phase and into a deeper, more realistic connection, actual emotional work begins. With time, both partners reveal their authentic selves and past wounds. Then, a question arises: how do we move forward from there?

Secure connection requires consistent work. Start here:

• Recognize that both partners have different needs and histories that shape their responses.
• Develop empathy for each other and learn about each other's triggers.
• Approach conflicts with a mindset of curiosity rather than blame.

People in secure relationships still have conflict; they just know how to repair ruptures when they happen.

In times of stress, it's helpful for couples to have proactive conversations about their emotional triggers and reactions. That way, they can later recognize when the other acts out of fear or hurt.

As a couple therapist, Baum offers key advice to her clients. Try this next time you find yourself in the middle of an escalating argument.

• Cool off before addressing the issue, and return to a calm state.
• Use "I statements" to express your feelings. Avoid blaming others.
• Listen reflectively. Mirror your partner's words to ensure you understand them correctly before responding.
• Validate the feelings of your partner even if you disagree with their arguments.

The main thing is to be patient. Building trust takes time. For example, when Baum met her second partner after an unfortunate first marriage, she told him it would take her five years to trust him fully. Through time, consistency, and communication, they built a relationship where she felt secure.

We will know we are moving into more healing when we have a different response to the old feelings arising. ~ Jessica Baum

Jessica managed to change her attachment style, and you can too. Even science supports this. Thanks to the brain's neuroplasticity, our attachment systems can change with new, supportive experiences. The more safety, empathy, and mutual vulnerability we experience in our relationships, the more secure we become.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
If at any point while reading this you thought, "Oh, that's me!" — don't panic. We all carry baggage from the past that affects our present relationship dynamics. The good news is that you're not alone in this.

By turning our attention inward and healing ourselves, we can become more self-full and ready to enter genuinely secure and healthy relationships. No, this is not a fairytale. Many people have managed to change their attachment styles and find partners with whom to build stronger bonds. So, you can, too!

Try this

• Take a minute to reflect on your attachment style and triggers.
• Identify your Inner Protector — what does it sound like in your head? Then, think of the people you've internalized as your Inner Nurturers.
• Write out your non-negotiables in relationships.
• Practice the "heart awareness" technique to stay grounded when emotionally disturbed.

Read it till the end?
